text: The UN innovation ecosystem can be mapped by four functions: entity-level implementation (WFP, UNICEF, UNDP, UNHCR, ITU, DPO), system-level exchange and knowledge (UNIN, UN 2.0, capacity-building), coordination/decision channels (DTN, HLCM/CEB, UN80 Steering Committee), and flexible delivery enablers (Global Pulse, UNICC, Digital ID/Data Commons, pooled funds).

Innovation at the UN is delivered through a mixed architecture of entity accelerators, labs, digital/data teams, programme-led pilots, pooled funds, communities of practice, and ad hoc inter-agency coalitions, with no single dominant model.

The UN innovation process follows a path from idea/demand signal to pilot/prototype to internal validation to attempted scale, with breakdowns at ownership, technical delivery, funding, procurement/legal, cross-entity resource sharing, and adoption transitions.

The UN system is strongest at entity-level innovation and distributed technical expertise, but critically lacks a mechanism for system-level implementation capacity that mobilizes entity resources for system-wide priorities.
Grassroots and entity innovations often fail to achieve multi-entity adoption due to lack of mandate, common owner, pooled funding, dedicated technical team, or sustainable service-provider route.
sourceIds: Talea interview (Global Pulse cohorts, Data Commons)

text: TAP can learn from at least five existing UN innovation mechanisms: WFP Innovation Accelerator, UNICEF Venture Fund, Global Pulse accelerator, Digital ID/Data Commons, and CRAF'd/MPTF pooled-funding model.

TAP should adopt substantive innovation practices including stage gates, fast sprints, adoption-owner checks, structured technical support, portfolio-level measurement, and clear transition routes to operations or cost recovery.

A TAP-worthy use case must pass four filters: it must be new or newly adapted, technology-enabled, have system-wide potential, and have a real adoption pathway with a named owner and measurable benefit.

Key barriers to UN innovation include fragmentation, funding constraints, policy/legal/governance hurdles, limited technical capacity, weak adoption ownership, earmarked funding, time scarcity, and culture/leadership gaps.
sourceIds: TAP survey slide in working draft

text: TAP support should vary by innovation maturity: Discovery (surveys, pipelines), Seed (sprint funding, technical assessment), Implementation (surge task forces, de-bottlenecking), Scale (fund adoption, connect adopters), and Transition (handover to service provider or cost-recovery mechanism).

TAP should prioritize use cases by triangulating multiple demand signals including business-needs surveys, existing pipelines, job postings, pooled-funding signals, and community inputs, not rely solely on a single survey.
TAP's primary client is the accountable problem or adoption owner—not just the innovator—who can commit to adoption, funding, legal/policy path, and transition.

A hybrid TAP delivery model should include a lean Coordination Unit, time-bound Surge Task Forces, the UN Innovation Ecosystem as knowledge source, a Steering Committee for portfolio decisions, and pooled funding with clear separation of governance functions.

TAP should operate as a catalytic venture with small seed tickets and larger scale-up funding after evidence and owner commitment, tracking KPIs on portfolio flow, adoption, and value—not idea volume.

The recommended transition path is to move from temporary UN80 governance into a standing joint programme or MPTF-style mechanism, progressing through mandate, demand, proposals, delivery, transition, and institutionalization.
